The Archaic through Hellenistic center seems to have been concentrated east of Hexamilia in the area of modern Kesimia (just west of Ag. Demetrios), with a possible subsequent Roman-era shift to the southwest around Monastiraki.

name: Kul'chukskoye
description: A fortified settlement in the chora of Chersonesos Taurike, dating from the Late Classical and Hellenistic periods, and reused in the early Medieval period.

name: Kūlfaraḥ
description: Place for Neo-elamite rock inscriptions.

name: Küllüoba Höyüğü
description: Küllüoba Höyüğü is the site of a large settlement mound dating from the Chalcolithic period and continuing to the Early Bronze Age.

name: Kumidi
description: Bronze age settlement.

name: Kūnārā
description: Bronze Age site with upper and lower towns and remains of a monumental building.

name: Kurşun Kalesi
description: Remains of an ancient hilltop settlement, with houses, agricultural processing areas, a stoa, a temple, and some ancient Greek inscriptions located within the modern Turkish municipality of Yeğenli (Mersin province). The site's ancient name is not known, but it has also been discussed under other modern names including Asar, İsmailli, and Kurşunlu.

name: Kuşsaray
description: A Bronze Age archaeological site consisting of a mound with finds of cuneiform tablets. The site was only briefly excavated.

name: Kyaneai
description: An ancient city of Lycia and the chief settlement in the region between Antiphellos and Myra.

name: Kyaneai (islands)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 53 B2 Kyaneai Inss.

name: Kythnos
description: The chief, homonymous city on the Cycladic island of Kythnos (modern Vriokastro/Episkopi).

name: L-Shaped Palace at Gla
description: A complex of multifunctional rooms at Gla with two connected wings that extend from a right angle to form two ʺmegaronsʺ that lacked hearths and throne rooms. The palace was built around 1300 BCE.

name: L’Ecluse
description: Remains of fortifications dating to the Late Roman period.

name: La Almoloya
description: La Almoloya, located in south-eastern Spain, is an archaeological site connected with the El Argar society of the Early Bronze Age. The site includes especially wealthy burials and a sprawling architectural complex that some scholars have equated with a sort of meeting hall or parliament.

name: La Cabañeta
description: A Roman-style settlement at modern El Burgo de Ebro, located on the south bank of the Ebro river, southeast of Zaragoza, Spain. The settlement appears to have been founded in the second century BCE and permanently destroyed in the first century, likely during the Sertorian War (82-72 BCE).

name: La Chapelle-des-Fougeretz
description: A Gallo-Roman settlement that persisted until the fourth century of the modern era at modern La Chapelle-des-Fougeretz. Known since the 20th century, rescue excavations ahead of a housing development on the southwest edge of the modern town were conducted in 2022 and revealed a sanctuary of Mars, a civic bath complex, and a late-antique necropolis. The site's ancient name(s) are unknown.

name: La Fonteta
description: Phoenician port located at the mouth of the Segura River on Spain's Mediterranean coast.

name: La Giostra
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 44 E2 La Giostra

name: La Hougue Bie
description: A Neolithic ritual site that includes a passage grave dating ca. 4000-3500 BCE.

name: Lacippo
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 E5 Lacippo

name: Lagash
description: Lagash was an important Sumerian city of the late third millennium B.C.

name: Lagina
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 61 G3 Lagina

name: Lamā Cemetery
description: Cemetery site near Lama village, Iran with almost 60 graves dating to the late 2nd-early 1st millennium CE. Most of the graves were destroyed by bulldozing in the area for a road in December 1999.

name: Laodicea ad Libanum
description: Laodicea ad Libanum was a Hellenistic city located on the Orontes River in Coele-Syria.

name: Lapethos/Lepethis Phoinikon
description: Located on the north coast of Cyprus 10 km west of Keryneia, Lapethos/Lepethis Phoinikon is known through archaeological remains for the most part. Archaeological evidence shows settlement already in the Late Bronze Age, continuing to the first millennium BCE and beyond. The tradition of Lapethos holds that the kingdom was founded by Praxandros from Lakonia in the Peloponnese.

name: Largo di Torre Argentina
description: A square in the Campus Martius of Rome, at the center of which lies an archaeological site that includes the remains of four temples of the Republican period, as well as elements of the first century B.C. theater complex of Cnaeus Pompeius.

name: Larsa
description: Larsa (modern Tell as-Senkereh), a city located ca. 21 km east of Uruk, was an important southern Mesopotamian cult center of the sun-god (Utu/Šamaš). Its principal religious structure was called Ebabbar, just like the Šamaš temple in the northern Babylonian city of Sippar. The Sumerian ceremonial names of some of Larsa’s other religious buildings are mentioned in two first-millennium-BC lists of ziggurats and some royal inscriptions, especially from the Isin-Larsa Period (ca. 2025–1763 BC) — when the city of Larsa temporarily became a dominant political power (ca. 1924–1763 BC) — and the Neo-Babylonian Period. The most important of these, at least in the sixth century BC, was the temple tower Edurana, which sometimes went by the name Eduranki. At the beginning of the second millennium BC (ca. 1924 BC), under a man called Gungunum (r. 1932–1906 BC), Larsa broke free of Isin’s dominance over it and established its political dynasty. Although it grew more powerful than its former overlord, Larsa never accumulated a significant amount of territory. At its peak, under the authority of Rīm-Sîn I (r. 1822–1763 BC), it controlled only ten to fifteen other city-states. This city’s political dominance, as well as its dynastic line, was brought to an end by Hammu-rāpi of Babylon (r. 1792–1750 BC). After 1763 BC, Larsa was never again a major political player in Babylonia.

name: Las Médulas
description: Las Médulas is the site of substantial Roman gold mines that have been listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

name: Lattara
description: A walled port city inhabited by Gauls, Etruscans, and Massalian Greeks.

name: Laureion
description: A mining region of Attika famous in antiquity for its silver mines. These mines were one of the chief sources of revenue for the Athenian state.

name: Lchashen
description: Urartian rock inscription near the town Lchashen.

name: Lederata
description: A Roman auxiliary fort on the Danube river at modern Ram (Serbia). It was reduced in size by the time of Diocletian and then renovated and expanded under Justinian.

name: Leibethra
description: An ancient settlement whose acropolis has been located at Kanalia, which is near the modern towns of Skotina and Leptokarya in the Greek Dion-Olympus municipality.

name: Leontini
description: A Greek colonial city founded by Naxians in 729 B.C., Leontini was reduced by Hippocrates of Gela in 498 B.C. Following the Sicilian Expedition, the city became subject to Syracuse. In Roman times it may have been of little importance and was destroyed by the Saracens in A.D. 848.

name: Leontion
description: An ancient settlement of Achaea in the northern Pelopnnese, the site is located on a hillside known today as Kastritsi at the northern foot of mount Erymanthos. It is not to be confused with the modern settlement of Leontio, which is located about 10 km north.

name: Lerna
description: Lerna was an ancient settlement on the east coast of the Peloponnesus. Extensive Bronze Age layers were excavated in the twentieth century by J. L. Caskey.

name: Les Cars
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 H3 Les Cars

name: Les Tours-Mirandes
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 F2 Les Tours-Mirandes

name: Letoa (island)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 60 C2 Letoa Ins.

name: Letoon
description: Sanctuary of Leto and center of the Lycian federation.

name: Leukai Inss.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 60 B2 Leukai Inss.

name: Leukos Limen
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 68 A2 Leukos Limen

name: Leuktron
description: Leuktron was an ancient settlement in the southern Peloponnese. Pausanias places it twenty stades from Pephnus.

name: Li Castelli
description: An ancient settlement of the Messapians located near the modern town of San Pancrazio Salentino on the Salento peninsula of Italy, the site was later used as a Roman military installation. The ancient name of the site is unknown.

name: Li Schiavoni (Nardò, Lecce)
description: Li Schiavoni is the site of a fortified Messapic center that flourished in the mid-first millennium BCE.

name: library at Nysa on the Maeander
description: A second-century CE library located at Nysa on the Maeander.

name: Library of Hadrian at Athens
description: The Library of Hadrian is located on the north side of the Athenian Acropolis, immediately to the north of the Roman Agora. The Roman emperor Hadrian built the complex in A.D. 131/2. It was subsequently destroyed during the Herulian sack of Athens in A.D. 267.

name: Library of Pergamum
description: The Attalid ruler Eumenes ll built and filled a library at Pergamum between 197 and 159 BCE. Nineteenth century excavators argued for the identification of a series of rooms behind the north stoa of the temple of Athena on the acropolis as housing this library, but more recent scholarship has called this identification into significant doubt (Coqueugniot 2013).

name: Lichades Islands
description: An island group off North Euboea, Greece. Near the Malian Gulf.

name: Lidar
description: Lidar or Lidar Höyük is an archaeological site located in southeastern Anatolia, to the northwest of Sanliurfa, Türkiye, and just southwest of the village of Lidar (district of Bozova). This settlement mound measured roughly 200x240 meters and stood 25 m high. Its remains are now inundated by the Atatürk Dam.

name: Limnaion
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 C1 Limnaion

name: Limyra
description: An ancient settlement located at modern Yuvalılar in the district of Finike, Antalya province, Turkey.

name: Lindinis
description: A Roman fort is thought to have been established at Lindinis (modern Ilchester) ca. 60 CE. The site protected the crossing of the Fosse Way over the River Yeo. The settlement developed in phases from the first to the fourth centuries CE. It was eventually one of only two known settlements with a stone wall in the territory of the Durotriges. The inhabitants of the town seem to have been reasonably wealthy, as a number of domestic complexes have fine mosaic pavements.

name: Little Palace at Knossos
description: The so-called "Little Palace at Knossos" lies some 200 m northwest of the main Minoan "palace" center and to the east of the Unexplored Mansion to which it was once linked.

name: Lixus
description: Lixus was an ancient settlement of Mauretania Tingitana. It was founded as a Punic port settlement as early as the eighth century B.C. Under the Roman emperor Claudius Lixus was elevated to the rank of colonia.

name: Lochmaben Stone
description: A standing megalith located near the mouth on the Solway Firth and dating ca. 3000 B.C.

name: Locmariaquer megaliths
description: At Locmariaquer, Brittany, are the remains of a complex of Neolithic structures.

name: Los Villares (Huétor-Tájar)
description: Los Villares (Huétor-Tájar) is an archaeological site located in the community of Los Villares (Huétor-Tájar). Here remains of a significant settlement dating to the third to sixth centuries CE have been discovered.

name: Lucone di Polpenazze
description: The archaeological site of Polpenazze del Garda (BS) Lucone is situated in a morainic basin in northern Italy. It is the site of several prehistoric settlements, which have been documented since archaeological excavations began in 2007. The Early Bronze Age settlement was active from ca. 2034 BCE until 1967 BCE. The site is included in the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Prehistoric Pile Dwellings around the Alps".

name: Ludus Magnus
description: A gladiatorial training school adjacent to the Flavian Amphitheater in Rome. The structure is attested on the Severan marble plan of Rome.

name: Luna
description: Luna (the modern Luni) is an ancient settlement of Liguria. It was re-founded by the Romans as a colonial settlement in 177 BC.

name: Luxmanda archaeological site
description: A large settlement in Tanzania dating to the Pastoral Neolithic period in eastern Africa.

name: Macedonian palace at Pella
description: The Macedonian palace at Pella, now only partially excavated, occupied an area of perhaps 60,000 square meters. The palace consisted of up to seven large architectural groupings.

name: Macellum (Pompeii)
description: The Macellum of Pompeii is located just outside the northeast corner of the Forum of the city.

name: Macellum of Puteoli
description: A macellum of Roman Puteoli. The discovery of a statue of Serapis at the site led to a misidentification of the building as a Serapeum.

name: Macellum of Sagalassos
description: The Roman Macellum of Sagalassos.

name: Magidû
description: Magidû or Megiddo (modern Tell al-Mutesellim, "The Tell of the Governor") is an ancient settlement mound located in northern Israel near Kibbutz Megiddo, about 30 km southeast of Haifa. Bronze Age Megiddo was an important Canaanite city-state and during the Iron Age, it was a royal city in the Kingdom of Israel. The site is mentioned in Neo-Assyrian sources.

name: Magnesia ad Maeandrum/Leukophrys
description: Magnesia ad Maeandrum was an ancient city on the Maeander River in Ionia, site of an important sanctuary of Artemis Leukophryene ("of the white eyebrow"). Its extensive remains are located near the modern village of Tekin in Turkey's Aydın Province.

name: Māhān
description: Tomb of Shah Nematollah Vali, find place for cuneiform inscription.

name: Maḥmūd Ābād
description: Mound and find place for a stone block with Urartian inscription.

name: Makistos
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 58 B2 Makistos

name: Makri St. peristyle
description: Remains of a Hellenistic stoa initially identified via salvage excavation in the 1960s and further explored ca. 2012. J. Travlos identified the remains as those of the Palladion court.

name: Malichou islands
description: A group of 10 major volcanic islands located in the Red Sea.

name: Mallia
description: Mallia is a site on the northern coast of Crete that was the location of a major Minoan palatial center of the Middle Bronze Age.

name: Malloura
description: Located at Malloura, Cyprus, are the remains of human settlement activity ranging from the Archaic period in the eastern Mediterranean to the time of the Roman Empire. The site has yielded evidence of a sanctuary with votive activity as well as funereal evidence. Modern exploration of the site began in 1990 under the direction of Michael K. Toumazou.

name: Mampsis
description: The Nabataean city of Mampsis or Memphis (Ancient Greek: Μέμφις) was an important station on the Incense Road.

name: Maniolai (islands)
description: Islands mentioned in Ptolemy. They may correspond to the modern Andaman islands in the Bay of Bengal.

name: mansio, via Tiburtina
description: A mansio located at the seventh mile of the Via Tiburtina.

name: Mantai
description: Mantai was an ancient coastal town and harbor of northern Sri Lanka.

name: Mardaman
description: Early and Middle Bronze Age city in northern Mesopotamia.

name: Māri
description: Mari (Tell Hariri) was an ancient Semitic city that flourished as a trade center and hegemonic state between 2900 BC until 1759 BC. The site was submitted as a tentative candidate for the UNESCO World Heritage list in 1999.

name: Mariana
description: Mariana was a Roman city on the east coast of Corsica founded by Caius Marius ca. 93 BC.

name: Marion
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 72 A2 Marion

name: Marlik
description: Burial site in northern Iran containing over fifty burials dated to the mid-2nd millennium BCE.

name: Marmachyn
description: Place for Urartian rock inscription, 1km from Marmashen Monastery.

name: Maşat Höyük
description: A Bronze Age Hittite center ca. 100 km east of Boğazkale/Hattusa.

name: Mashkan-Shapir
description: Mashkan-Shapir, covering 56 ha, was occupied during the Uruk period and becomes more prominent during the Akkadian period and then during the Ur III period. The site is known today as Tall Abū Duwārī.

name: Matara
description: Matara, in Eritrea, is an archaeological site located along the coast road to Adulis and dating to pre-Aksumite or early Aksumite times. It is possibly to be identified as the Koloe mentioned in a Roman imperial source.

name: Me-Turnat
description: Me-Turnat was a Bronze Age and Iron Age settlement located on the Diyala river.

name: Medeon
description: Medeon was a polis of ancient Greece with occupation stretching from the Archaic period to Late Antiquity. It is located southeast of Aspra Spitia, Viotia, Central Greece.

name: Mediana
description: An extensive late-Roman villa used as a temporary imperial residence in the suburbs of ancient Naissus. Located in the modern village of Brzi brod, within the municipality of Niš, Serbia (BAtlas 21 E6 Mediana).

name: Medina Azahara
description: The Caliphate City of Medina Azahara is a city built in the mid-tenth century CE by the Umayyad dynasty as the seat of the Caliphate of Cordoba.

name: Medinet Bu Hindi
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 38 D1 Medinet Bu Hindi

name: Megali Koryfi
description: Megali Koryfi, an archaeological site on the island of Aegina in proximity to the Christian chapel of Ag. Antonios that is noted for the presence of substantial remains of drystone fortifications dating to the Mycenaean period.

name: Megali Magoula
description: A Middle to Late Bronze Age settlement located on the hill of Megali Magoula that includes Mycenaean chamber tombs.

name: Megalopolis
description: An ancient settlement in Arcadia in the Peloponnese, likely founded in the 4th century BCE. The site lies just to the northeast of the modern town of Megalopolis.

name: Megalopolis Lignite Mines
description: Through a series of survey and rescue excavations, the modern lignite mines just west of modern Megalopolis (and southwest of the ancient site of Megalopolis) in the Peloponnese have produced important evidence for ice age activity, especially at (but not limited to) a Lower Paeleolithic site known as Marathoussa 1.

name: Megaron A at Thermon
description: Megaron A is an apsidal megaron built sometime between 1400 BC and 1100 BC in the area of the later Sanctuary of Apollo at Thermon.

name: Mehrgarh
description: The archaeological site of Mehrgarh covers an area of some 250 ha. It is located in the Kachi plain (Balochistan, Pakistan) near the Bolan Pass. The Neolithic-period site consists of numerous archaeological mounds.

name: Melabron?
description: A Hellenistic-and-Roman-era site, sometimes locally referred to as Palaiokastro, on the northwest coast of Cyprus, west of the modern village of Ag. Eirini. Archaeological investigation also produced evidence of Bronze Age habitation here. The historical site is possibly to be identified with the attested name Melabron.

name: Melanteioi Skopeloi (island)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 61 B3 Melanteioi Skopeloi

name: Memphis
description: A city and capital of Ancient Egypt that remained an important center for most of Egyptian history.

name: Menariae (islands)
description: Pliny the Elder describes the Menariae insulae (Menariae Islands) as an island group located in the Bay of Palma, off the coast of the island of Mallorca.

name: Mendolito
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 47 F3 Mendolito

name: Ménec alignments
description: A complex comprised of 11 rows of megaliths covering an area of 1,165 x 100 meters.

name: Meninx (settlement)
description: A harbor settlement (in the vicinity of modern El Kantara) in the southern part of the Tunisian island of Djerba (ancient Meninx/Lotophagitis/Girba).

name: Mensa Ponderaria
description: The Mensa ponderaria (VII,7,31) was an official public office adjacent to the forum of Pompeii wherein weights and measures were regulated. This location was in use from the late second century BC.

name: Mentorides (islands)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 20 B5 Mentorides Inss.

name: Methydrion
description: An ancient city in northern Arcadia, located near the modern Greek town that now bears its name (formerly Nemnitsa).

name: Metropolis
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 56 E5 Metropolis

name: Metropolis (Thessaly)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 B2 Metropolis

name: Metropolis? (settlement)
description: The modern village of Palaiomanina in the West Greek regional unit of Aetolia-Acarnania may be identifiable with "Metropolis", a settlement known from ancient sources.

name: Metsamor
description: A prehistoric site with megalithic standing stones located to the southwest of the Armenian village of Taronik (Armavir Province).

name: Metsopay-Vank
description: Armenian monastery and find place for a Urartian stele.

name: Meturant
description: Hamrin basin site named Tall al-Sīb.

name: Mezraa Teleilat
description: A low mound on the east bank of the Euphrates, close to the border between Syria and Turkey. Salvage excavations were undertaken here around the year 2000. The site has yielded cuneiform finds.

name: Midhowe
description: A large Neolithic chambered cairn located on the island of Rousay, Orkney, Scotland.

name: Mieza
description: An ancient Macedonian settlement, located between Beroia and Edessa (between the modern villages of Lefkadia and Kopanos). Ancient sources report that it was here that Philip built facilities at which Aristotle taught the young Alexander and his colleagues.

name: Mieza Nymphaion
description: The Mieza Nymphaion is a possible site of Aristotle's school, located between Kopanos and Naousa, Imathia, Macedonia, Greece.

name: Mikhmoret
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 A5 Mikhmoret

name: Milatos
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 60 E2 Milatos

name: Miletus
description: A major ancient city in Ionia.

name: Mineral Water Hospital site
description: Remains of a large building discovered in Aquae Sulis. The site now lies on the grounds of the Royal National Hospital for Rheumatic Diseases.

name: Minturnae
description: Originally an Ausonian town that fought against Rome in 314 B.C., Minturnae became a colony in 296 B.C. The site is located on the Garigliano river (ancient Liris) and served an important economic function. The Via Appia passes through the city.

name: Mira Lanza
description: The remains of an Archaic period structure at Mira Lanza, located in Via Luigi Pierantoni.

name: Moenjodaro
description: An ancient (third millennium BC) city of the Indian sub-continent connected with the Harappan Civilization. It was designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1980.

name: Mola di Monte Gelato
description: An ancient settlement 30 km north of Rome that was excavated from 1986-90. An imperial villa existed on the site up until the third century AD.

name: Molpa
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 46 B1 Molpa

name: Molykreion?
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 B4 Molykreion?

name: Monastiraki Katalimata
description: A refuge settlement in Crete located near the Late Minoan site of Chalasmenos.

name: Monemvasia
description: A Medieval refuge settlement founded ca. AD 583 that developed into an important center from the tenth century onward.

name: Mont'e Prama
description: A locality near Cabras, Sardinia, where, in 1974, the cache of monumental prehistoric stone sculptures known as the "Giants of Mont'e Prama" was discovered.

name: Monte Baranta
description: A pre-nuragic architectural complex near Olemdo, Sardinia.

name: Monte Croce Guardia
description: Monte Croce Guardia (province of Ancona) was a site of human settlement in the Recent Bronze Age (first half of the twelfth century BCE) that went on to flourish during the Final Bronze Age (ca. 1150-925 BCE). The settlement once covered an area of more than 22 ha.

name: Monte Finocchito
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 47 G5 Monte Finocchito

name: Monte Maranfusa
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 47 C3 Monte Maranfusa

name: Monte Pruno
description: A settlement of the Oenotrians and the Lucani dating to the seventh through third centuries B.C. Its ancient name is unknown.

name: Monte Savello (Rome)
description: Monte Savello is an area located in Rome's Sant'Angelo district, between the pons Fabricius and the Capitoline Hill. Its status as 'monte' is owed to the fact that an artificial mound of debris accumulated in the area of the Circus Flaminius and the Theater of Marcellus, a mound that came to serve as a de facto quarry for spoliated building materials. "Savello" refers to the post-eleventh century incorporation of the area within the fortifications of the Savello family. Modern excavations have revealed Roman, post-Roman, Medieval, and more recent settlement horizons.

name: Monte Sirai
description: A Phoenician–Punic and later Roman settlement in southwestern Sardinia, Monte Sirai also has evidence for nuragic settlement during the second millennium BC.

name: Monte Testaccio
description: An artificial mountain on the banks of the Tiber River at Rome composed of 580,000 cubic meters of broken amphorae.

name: Monte Turcisi
description: Monte Turcisi is the site of Greek fortifications located in the province of Catania, Sicily. This military outpost was likely of Syracusan dependence.

name: Monte Vairano
description: At Monte Vairano are the remains of a fortified center of the Pentrian Samnites.

name: Monterfil II at Corseul
description: Remains of the commercial quarter of the Gallo-Roman settlement.

name: Montesei di Serso
description: A hilltop settlement with a long-term habitation, stretching from ca. 2000 BC to the first century BC. The archaeological site preserves the remains of 4+ mid-first millennium BC dwellings.

name: Monument of Kallimachos
description: A column topped by a winged female figure on the Acropolis of Athens, dedicated in 490 BC by the polemarch Kallimachos to commemorate his victory at the Battle of Marathon. It was destroyed in the Persian Sack of 480 BC.

name: Monumental Arch of Palmyra
description: The Monumental Arch of Palmyra was a third-century CE arched gateway built at Palmyra. Terrorists of the so-called Islamic State destroyed it in 2015.

name: Monumental complex of Sant'Ilario a Port' Aurea
description: An architectural complex associated with the nearby Longobard church of Sant'Ilario. The complex included a monastery and is built atop Roman Imperial remains.

name: Monumental Mid-Republican Building Complex
description: This monumental complex of buildings at the city of Gabii was likely constructed in the mid-third century BCE as a public area for feasts, bathing, and ritual activities.

name: Morería
description: The Morería archaeological area preserves the remains of the multiple urban phases of the site of Mérida.

name: Morgantina
description: An indigenous Sicilian site founded by the Morgeti, according to Strabo, Morgantina was eventually defeated by Syracuse and thus, in turn, passed into Roman hegemony in the third century B.C.

name: Motya
description: A small island in the Stagnone Lagoon of western Sicily, Motya has evidence for quite ancient settlement, including a significant Punic phase. The settlement on the island was destroyed by Syracuse in 379 BC. At one point the island was connected to the mainland via a causeway.

name: Mound at Banāt al-Hassan (Adams 100)
description: Part of a group of mounds in central Mesopotamia, this settlement (number 100 in Adams 1972) containing remains dated from the Akkadian through Larsa periods.

name: Mound at Banāt al-Hassan (Adams 131)
description: Part of a group of mounds in central Mesopotamia, this settlement (number 131 in Adams 1972) contained remains dated to the Early Dynastic I-Old Babylonian periods (chiefly Ur III-Larsa). The site also contains a number of burials from the Parthian period, fragments of Sasanian pottery, and the remains of an Early Islamic structure.

name: Msletten II (fortified farm)
description: A fortified villa rustica located along the desert road from Banu Walid to Qasr Banat and Ghirza in Libya.

name: Muhattet el-Haj (lower)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 71 B3 Muhattet el-Haj (lower)

name: Mukdan
description: Mound (Umm al-Jīr) and find place for Akkadian cuneiform tablets.

name: Murat Höyük
description: Settlement on the Murat River in Eastern Anatolia inhabited in the Early Bronze Age (ca. 2500-2200 BCE), the Early-Middle Iron Age, and the Medieval Period. It is located near, but distinct from, the Urartian and Byzantine site of Murat Tepe.

name: Murat Tepe
description: Settlement on the Murat River inhabited during the Urartian and Byzantine periods. It is located near, but distinct from, the Bronze Age-Medieval site of Murat Höyük.

name: Murayghat
description: An early Bronze Age site in modern Jordan, located about 12 km southwest of Madaba. The site has been interpreted as a ceremonial meeting place for regional groups, featuring a central area with monumental structures and standing stones surrounded by dolmen fields, which may mark burial sites.

name: Murgia Timone
description: The site of a Neolithic village located in the territory of Matera in southern Italy. The site was initially discovered in the 1890s by Domenico Ridola.

name: Must Farm Bronze Age settlement
description: A well-preserved pile-dwelling settlement site dating to the Late Bronze Age that is located in Cambridgeshire, United Kingdom.

name: Muweilah
description: A fortified settlement dating to the Iron Age II period (ca. 1,100-600 BCE).

name: Mylai (islands)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 60 A1 Mylai Inss.

name: Myndos
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 61 E3 Myndos

name: Myous
description: An ancient Ionian port settlement in Caria, the site is located at modern Avşar Kale in Turkey. The site was abandoned in antiquity due to siltification from the Maeander river; it was modestly resettled in the Byzantine period.

name: Myra
description: An ancient Lycian town

name: Myrina
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 56 A2 Myrina

name: Myrina/Sebastopolis
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 56 D4 Myrina/Sebastopolis

name: Myrmekes
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 56 D4 Myrmekes

name: Myrmekion
description: An ancient Greek colony in Crimea. The site is located in the modern eastern part of Kerch, known as Karantinnaya.

name: Mystras
description: A fortified town in Laconia, Peloponnese, Greece, Mystras was named as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1989.

name: Nabulu
description: A town mentioned in Neo-Assyrian sources that is now identified as modern Girnavaz Höyük. Nabulu was a Late Bronze Age and an Iron Age settlement located north of Nusaybin.

name: Nad-i Ali/Zarang?
description: A large site in modern Afghanistan's Nimruz province. Archaeological evidence is abundant for habitation during the third - first millennia BCE, as well as 8th - 13th centuries CE, when Nād ʿAlī was clearly a major fortified urban center.

name: Najafabad
description: Najafabad (Persian: نجف‌آباد‎) is a city in Iran and the capital of Najafabad, Isfahan Province. The discovery of a stele of the Assyrian king Sargon II (r. 721-705 BC) indicates that the settlement’s ancient name, at least in the eighth century BC, might have been Kišesim.

name: Namrīk
description: Site excavated in conjunction with the Mosul Dam project, currently submerged.

name: Naqa
description: Naqa is an ancient city located approximately 170 km northeast of Khartoum in modern-day Sudan. Naqa was part of the Kushitic Kingdom of Meroë.

name: Naulochon/Smyrna/Palaia Smyrna
description: An ancient settlement located on the gulf of Smyrna, east of the town of Bayraklı (some 4 km north of Izmir, Turkey). Archaeological evidence indicates that the earliest Hellenic evidence dates to the eleventh or tenth centuries B.C.

name: Nausharo
description: Archaeological site in Balochistan, Pakistan, associated with the Indus Valley civilization. The site shows signs of habitation from the early third to the early second millennium BCE.

name: Neapolis/Lepcis Magna
description: Originally a Phoenician colony founded ca. 1100 B.C., Lepcis Magna became a prominent Roman city and birthplace of the emperor Septimius Severus.

name: Nebelivka
description: Nebelivka is a Neolithic-period archaeological site in Ukraine.

name: Neolithic Settlement at Knossos
description: A neolithic settlement on a hill known today as Kephala, south of Heraklion on the island of Crete. It preceded the Bronze Age (Minoan) palace and settlement on this site.

name: Neonteichos
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 56 E4 Neonteichos

name: Nerik
description: Nerik was a Bronze Age, Hittite settlement. It is likely to be identified with the Oymaağaç Höyük in northern Turkey.

name: Nestane
description: An ancient fortified settlement in Arcadia.

name: New Bouleuterion
description: The New Bouleuterion was the meeting place of the Boule or Council of Athens, built at the end of the fifth century BC on the west side of the Agora, where it formed part of a complex with the Old Bouleuterion and the Tholos. It was modified in the early third century BC and destroyed in the Herulian Sack of AD 267.

name: Nikasiai (islands)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 61 B3 Nikasiai Inss.

name: Nimrud
description: In the ninth century BC, Ashurnasirpal II (r. 883-859 BC) built himself a new administrative center. Kalhu (biblical Calah) — modern Nimrud, a site identified as Larissa, a city mentioned in Xenophon’s Anabasis — became the capital of the Assyrian Empire, replacing Ashur, which had served as Assyria’s capital since the third millennium BC. The city — which is located twenty miles south of the modern Mosul and which occupied a strategic position six miles north of the point where the Tigris River meets the Greater/Upper Zab — served as Assyria’s capital until the reign of Sargon II (r. 721–705 BC). Although Kalhu never again became the primary residence of Assyria’s kings, that city remained a vital administrative, military, and religious center until the fall of the Assyrian Empire.

name: Nina
description: Tell Zurghul is an archaeological site in Iraq covering approximately 70 ha. The site includes two settlement mounds. The ancient site was located on a canal leading away from Lagash, some 10 km to the southeast. The ancient name of this place was Niĝin (or Nina or Ninua), and Nanshe (Nanše) was the titular divinity.

name: Nineveh Archive 1
description: The Palace of Sennacherib (South-West Palace) at Nineveh remained in use until the Assyrian capital was captured, looted, and destroyed in 612 BC. Numerous tablets discovered in Rooms XL and XLI, as well as in some of the adjoining rooms, nineteenth-century British excavators unearthed the remains of a library, including many clay tablets inscribed with literary texts. During the reign of Ashurbanipal (r. 668–ca. 631 BC), the last great king of Assyria, the tablets found in this archive repository were probably part of his tablet collection.

name: Nineveh Archive 2
description: In the Palace of Sennacherib (South-West Palace) at Nineveh, in Room LXI, nineteenth-century British excavators discovered about 450 clay bullae. Most of these had seal impressions on one side and indications on their backsides that they were fastened with rope to perishable materials (for example, papyrus and leather). These bullae are probably the remains of an archive of Aramaic documents.

name: Nineveh Archive 3
description: In the Palace of Sennacherib (South-West Palace) at Nineveh, in Room LIV, nineteenth-century British excavators found the remains of an archive consisting of cuneiform tablets, some of which also included summary notes in Aramaic.

name: Nineveh Archive 4
description: In the Palace of Ashurbanipal (North Palace), in Room C, nineteenth-century British excavators unearthed the remains of an archive of clay tablets. This find yielded records for military officers and possibly literary texts (although this has yet to be conclusively proven).

name: Nineveh Archive 5
description: In the Palace of Ashurbanipal (North Palace), in the southwest corner nineteenth-century British excavators unearthed the remains of a library, including many clay tablets inscribed with literary texts. During the reign of Ashurbanipal (r. 668–ca. 631 BC), the last great king of Assyria, the tablets found in this archive repository were probably part of his tablet collection. Because these tablets were excavated very close to the Nabû temple (Ezida), which also had its own archival repository, some of the tablets thought to have come from the so-called “Library of Ashurbanipal” might have actually belonged to the neighboring Nabû temple. Today, the North Palace library material is not clearly differentiated from that of Ezida, due to the fact that the excavators did not provide detailed information about the exact findspots of the tablets.

name: Nineveh Archive 6
description: A clay pot with about thirty clay tablets was found to the north of the Šamaš Gate at Nineveh, in a secondary context. The archive, whose texts are dated between 669 BC and the fall of Nineveh (612 BC), belonged to Ninurta-šarru-uṣur (“the son of the palace”), as well as to several other people, including some Egyptians. This small archive consists of loan and purchase documents, legal texts, as well as a piece of correspondence.

name: Nineveh/Ninos
description: This important Mesopotamian city flanks the eastern edge of the Tigris flood plain, opposite modern Mosul, of which it is now a suburb. From the third millennium B.C. onwards, Nineveh was the most important religious center of the goddess Ištar in the area that would become the Assyrian heartland. Starting in the Middle Assyrian period, the city came under the authority of the kings of Assyria, who often sponsored large-scale building activities there. However, it was not until 704 B.C. that Nineveh became the administrative capital of Assyria when the Neo-Assyrian king Sennacherib moved the royal family and court there and transformed the city into a thriving imperial metropolis. Nineveh remained Assyria’s capital until 612 B.C., when it was captured and destroyed by a Babylonian-Median coalition led by Nabopolassar and Cyaxares. The visible remains of the (7th-century) Assyrian city include the citadel mound Kuyunjik, the smaller mound of Nebi Yunus, and the twelve-kilometer-long city wall.

name: Nippur/Nufar/‘Hippareni’
description: An ancient Sumerian settlement whose ruins lie at Nuffar in modern Iraq. Under various names it persisted as a settlement throughout the periods of Greek and Roman engagement in Babylonia.

name: Nisaki
description: The remains of Classical to Hellenistic fortifications on the islet Nisaki Agion Anargyron.

name: Nisyrion (islands)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 61 E4 Nisyrion Inss.

name: Noiodounon Diablintum
description: Noiodounon Diablintum is a settlement with significant extant remains, located at modern Jublains in France.

name: Norşuntepe
description: Norşuntepe is the site of a Chalcolithic and Iron Age tell located in Turkey's Elazığ Province. Excavations led by Harald Hauptmann between 1968 and 1974 identified 40-some occupational horizons stretching from the fifth millennium BCE to the Iron Age. It is now partially inundated by Lake Keban.

name: Noues
description: Pottery workshop site in Gaul near the modern village of La Boissière-École containing several workshop sites used from the first-third centuries CE.

name: Novyy Kumak
description: Burial ground close to the modern city Orsk, possibly covered by modern construction.

name: Numeira
description: Archaeological site on the eastern shore of the Dead Sea with signs of habitation dated to the Early Bronze Age.

name: Numluli
description: A major settlement, cited: BAtlas 32 D3 Numluli

name: Nuraghe la Prisciona
description: A nuragic site in northeast Sardinia, it was occupied from the fourteenth until the ninth century BC. At its height, the complex covered approximately five hectares.

name: Nuraghe S'Urachi
description: A nuragic site near San Vero Milis in west central Sardinia, occupied from the Late Bronze Age into the Roman period.

name: Nymphaeum of via degli Annibaldi
description: An elaborate nymphaeum on the Oppian Hill discovered in 1895. The complex dates to the Late Republican period. The ancient nymphaeum was partially obliterated by the construction of the Neronian Domus Aurea.

name: Nymphaion
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 87 K2 Nymphaion

name: Octagonal hall, Villa dei Gordiani
description: A Roman structure with three levels that was re-purposed in the Middle Ages, the so-called Octagonal hall at the Villa dei Gordiani was likely originally either a nymphaeum or part of a bath complex.

name: Odèon/Ekklesiasterion
description: The Bouleuterion of ancient Region (modern Reggio Calabria).

name: Oescus
description: Oescus was originally a Thracian city at the confluence of the Danube and Iskar rivers,

name: Oichalia?
description: Oechalia in Euboea, possible archaeological traces of which may be found at the site of Kastri/Ano Potamia.

name: Oinoe (Attica)
description: An ancient fortified settlement and deme of Attica. The archaeological site is located to the east of modern Oinoe/Inoi (formerly Mazi).

name: Oinous(s)ai (islands)
description: A group of seven islands in the Aegean, located between the island of Chios and the Karaburun peninsula of Asia Minor (modern Turkey).

name: Oinoussai (islands)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 58 B4 Oinoussai Inss.

name: Olbia
description: Olbia was a large fortified settlement established by Massilia on the Mediterranean coast east of the Rhône river at the end of the fourth century BC.

name: Olcinium/Vicinium
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 49 B2 Olcinium/Vicinium

name: Old Kandahar citadel
description: Tradition holds that the Iron Age and Achaemenid citadel of Old Kandahar was re-established during the time of Alexander the Great. It functioned as a local seat of power for at least two millennia.

name: Olynthus, House A 11
description: House A 11 describes the archaeological remains of an irregularly planned house located in ancient Olynthus. These Olynthian houses date after 432 BCE and are destroyed in 348 BCE.

name: Omboi
description: The Ptolemaic temple to Sobek and Horus the Elder at Kom Ombo.

name: Opis
description: Opis was an ancient city of Babylonia located on the Tigris River close to the site of modern Baghdad.

name: Oppidum du Castellas de St Martin
description: An oppidum located in the vicinity of the modern commune in the Alpes-Maritimes department of southeastern France.

name: Orcades (islands)
description: Orcades Inss. (The Orkney Islands), an archipelago of northern Scotland comprised of ca. 70 islands.

name: Ordu-Baliq
description: Ordu-Baliq, also known as Mubalik and Karabalghasun, was once the capital of the Uyghur Khaganate. The ruins of this settlement are now included as part of the Orkhon Valley Cultural Landscape World Heritage Site in Mongolia.

name: Orikon
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 49 B3 Orikon

name: Orippo
description: A Roman city with Turdetanian origins. It is cited on both the Antonine Itinerary and the Vicarello goblets. Based on these itineraries it is to be located between Ugia (Torre Alocaz, Utrera) and Hispalis (Seville), near the banks of the Lacus Ligustinus.

name: Osteria di Centocelle
description: An nymphaeum located at Osteria di Centocelle dating to the Roman Imperial period.

name: Ostia
description: The port city of ancient Rome located at the mouth of the river Tiber.

name: Oued Beht
description: A large, prehistoric site in modern Morocco's Khémisset province that, at the time of systematic excavations published in 2024, constitutes the "earliest and largest agricultural complex in Africa beyond the Nile corridor". The site (which lies on a long ridge of high ground) gets its name from the Baht River, which runs along the eastern edge and separates the site from the neighboring modern town, which also carries the same name.

name: Oxeiai/Thoai (islands)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 54 D5 Oxeiai/Thoai Inss.

name: Oylum
description: Oylum Höyük is a settlement mound that was inhabited from the fourth millennium BC until the Hellenistic period.

name: Ozbaki
description: Chalcolithic site, find place for cuneiform inscriptions.

name: Padakku/Fadak
description: Ancient Padakku/Fadak is an oasis town in the Arabian Peninsula that is idenified with the modern city al-Ḥā’iṭ.

name: Paedagogium
description: The so-called Pedagogium is a building that was part of the Flavian palace complex on the Palatine Hill. It was located below the exedra of the façade of the Domus Augustana. The Paedagogium is thought to have superseded the late-Republican domus known as the Domus Gelotiana.

name: Pagus Sarclanus
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 44 E3 Pagus Sarclanus

name: Palace G at Ebla
description: Palace at Ebla dated to the early Bronze Age (24th century BCE), built atop the remains of an older palace (designated G2). Palace G, destroyed around 2300 BCE, contained an extensive archive of clay tablets.

name: Palace of Ardashir
description: A castle built in A.D. 224 by King Ardashir I of the Sassanian Empire, the Palace of Ardashir is located 2 km north of Gor.

name: Palace of Ashurbanipal
description: The Neo-Assyrian king Ashurbanipal built a grand royal residence in the citadel of Nineveh. It was located north and east of the temples of the god Nabû (Ezida) and the goddess Ištar (Emašmaš), and it was constructed on the site of an older, smaller palace. Ashurbanipal referred to this palace as the bīt redûti ("House of Succession"); in scholarly literature, it is often referred to as the "North Palace." A small portion of the once-grand Assyrian palace is still visible today.

name: Palace of Diocletian
description: A third-century A.D. fortified palace built by the emperor Diocletian.

name: Palace of Mausolos at Bodrum
description: The fortified Palace of Mausolos at Bodrum.

name: Palace of Naqi'a
description: The influential Neo-Assyrian queen Naqi’a built a royal residence for her son Esarhaddon in the citadel of Nineveh. This palace, which may have served as a summer residence for the Assyrian king and his mother, remains unlocated. It is certain from contemporary Akkadian inscriptions that the palace was constructed near the Sîn-Šamaš temple, but the exact location of that temple is not known.

name: Palace of Nebuchadnezzar II
description: The Neo-Babylonian king Nebuchadnezzar II constructed for himself a large and ornately decorated palace, the ruins of which are still visible today. This extensive architectural complex was located in the Kadingirra district of Babylon, which is situated just south of the Ishtar Gate, east of the processional street Ay-ibūr-šabû, north of the Lībil-hengalla canal, and west of the Euphrates River. Several Akkadian inscriptions of Nebuchadnezzar II record the construction of this building.

name: Palace of Sennacherib
description: The Neo-Assyrian king Sennacherib built a magnificent royal residence in the citadel of Nineveh. This extensive architectural complex was given the Sumerian ceremonial name Egalzagdinutukua, which means "Palace Without a Rival." A small portion of the once-grand Assyrian palace is still visible today.

name: Palace of the Shirvanshahs
description: A fifteenth-century palace built by the Shirvanshahs in Baku, Azerbaijan.

name: Palace of Tupkish
description: Palace at the Hurrian city Urkesh, constructed by Tupkish in the late third millennium BCE.

name: Palaiokastro
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 57 B6 Palaiokastro

name: Palairos
description: A fortified ancient Greek city located in southern Epirus.

name: Palamari
description: A fortified site located on the island of Skyros with Early Bronze Age activity.

name: Palatianon
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 50 C2 Palatianon

name: Palatine Stadium
description: "Palatine Stadium" is likely a misnomer when considering the probable function of this architectural complex located on the southeast corner of the Palatine Hill on the flank of the Domus Augustana-Flavia. Originating during the reign of the Domitian, this complex was restored numerous times, including during the reigns of Hadrian and Septimius Severus. As far as function goes, it may have been an elaborate, porticoed leisure garden.

name: Palatium Sessorianum
description: Palatium Sessorianum is of unknown origins. It was situated at the extreme south-east of Region V and it adjoined the amphitheatrum Castrense. It pre-dated the Aurelian wall. The portions of the complex within the line of the walls was an imperial residence by the time of Constantine.

name: Palazzo Imperiale (Ostia)
description: The so-called Palazzo Imperiale at Ostia is located at the western margin of Regio I. This building complex was initially excavated between 1855 and 1871 and many of its finds, especially mosaics, were transported to the Vatican Museum. The complex dates to the second and third centuries CE and includes a variety of installations - shops, storehouses, bathing facilities - but is most likely not a palace despite its modern sobriquet.

name: Palazzo Valentini
description: Rome's Palazzo Valentini was first built in 1585 on the site of a pre-existing palazzo located adjacent to what was then the piazza dei Santi Apostoli. The palazzo's foundations are entangled with various ancient remains, including perhaps architectural elements of the Forum of Trajan.

name: Paleoskala
description: A prehistoric settlement on the shores of the ancient Boebeis Lake.

name: Palike
description: An ancient settlement in east central Sicily, atop the Rocchicella, which included a sanctuary to the Divine Palikoi (a pair of boiling lakes) within a grotto found on the hillside.

name: Pallantion
description: An ancient settlement located just west of the modern Greek city that has borne the ancient name since the 20th century (previously Berbati). Archaeological evidence confirms continuous habitation from the archaic through the Byzantine periods, and there is plentiful evidence in the surrounding area for activity beginning in the Mycenaean period.

name: Pallanum
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 42 G4 Pallanum

name: Palmatis
description: Palmatis was a road station in Moesia Inferior. On the Peutinger map, it lies along the route between Durostorum and Marcianopolis. The archaeological remains of this place have been identified near Tervel, Bulgaria.

name: Palmyra
description: Palmyra is an ancient oasis and trading city with monumental remains located in modern Syria. Under Zenobia, It was the capital of the breakaway Palmyrene Empire, which led to Aurelian razing the city. In 1980, Palmyra was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

name: Panopeos/Phanotis
description: A city of Phocis on the Boeotian frontier. Substantive remains of the ancient walled city are visible on the height immediately south of modern Hagios Vlasios (Municipality of Livadia, Boeotia, Greece).

name: Papoura Hill Circular Structure
description: A Minoan architectural complex that consists of sets of concentric stone circles, the Papoura Hill Circular Structure is located on Papoura Hill, northwest of Kastelli Crete. The structure is dated ca. 2000 -1700 BCE.

name: Parco Archeologico Sommerso di Baia
description: The underwater archaeological park of Baia is a protected marine and cultural heritage area. Submerged beneath the waters of the Gulf of Naples are various Roman-period archaeological remains, including those of harbors, villas, shrines, and nymphaea.

name: Parishan tepe
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 98 G2 Parishan tepe

name: Park of the Roman Villas at Carthage
description: An excavated section of Roman Carthage containing blocks (insulae) of townhouses organized on a grid.

name: Parque Arqueológico El Molinete
description: An extensive urban archaeological area (more than 26,000 square meters) in Cartagena with archaeological horizons stretching from the third century BCE to the twentieth century CE.

name: Pasargadae/Batrakatash
description: Pasargadae/Batrakatash was an ancient Persian city and the capital of Cyrus the Great (559–530 BC). It remained the capital until Cambyses II moved it to Susa. The site is listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

name: Patibīri
description: A town mentioned in Neo-Assyrian sources; an alternate name is Bad-tibira; the modern site is Tel Madain.

name: Pausilipum
description: Pausilipum was an ancient Graeco-Roman settlement on the Bay of Naples.

name: Paxoi (islands)
description: Paxoi Inss., the smallest group of Ionian islands. Paxos and Antipaxos lie 13 km south of Corcyra.

name: Pegai/Antipatris/Arethousa?
description: A series of settlements and fortifications localized in and around the mound known today as Tel Afek/Râs el-'Ain in Israel. The mound itself is dominated today by the remains of an Ottoman fort, built on Crusader foundations, but an excavated Roman-era odeon can also be seen.

name: Pella/Apamea
description: Pella/Apamea in Syria was an ancient city established by the Seleucids ca. 300 B.C.

name: Pella/Berenice/Philippeia
description: Pella/Berenice/Philippeia (Tabaqat Fahl (طبقة فحل)) is a ruined ancient city located in northwestern Jordan. Pella was continuously occupied from the Neolithic period until its destruction by means of an earthquake in AD 749.

name: Pella/Diokletianoupolis
description: Capital of the Kingdom of Macedonia.

name: Pelusium
description: Pelusium was a fortified ancient city located at the mouth of the eastern-most branch of the Nile river. Its ruins, now land-locked and located approximately 30km southeast of Port Said, are known as Tell Farama.

name: Perge
description: An ancient Greek city of Asia Minor and capital of Pamphylia.

name: Persepolis/Parsa/Persai/Sat Setun
description: Persepolis/Parsa/Persai/Sat Setun was the ceremonial capital of the Achaemenid Empire. The origins of the settlement belong to the sixth century BC (ca. 515). The archaeological site was named as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1979.

name: Persian and Hellenistic Administrative Building (PHAB), Tel Kedesh
description: The Persian and Hellenistic Administrative Building was constructed at Tel Kedesh by the Persians in the mid-4th century BCE and was in use through the middle Hellenistic Period (around 143 BCE).

name: Persou (Wadi Hammamat)
description: Wadi Hammamat was the site of major mining and quarrying operations in the ancient world. The site is mentioned in the Turin Papyrus Map, in connection with a quarrying expedition under Ramesses IV.

name: Peschiera
description: The so-called Peschiera is an architectural complex on the grounds of Hadrian's Villa at Tivoli.

name: Peshwaran
description: An archaeological site located in Afghanistan's Nimruz Province, some 25 km south of Lash Juwain. Materials on the site ate to the period between the ninth and thirteenth centuries. A large archaeological complex estimated at 9.5 by 13 km has been documented, containing numerous smaller sites. A large citadel is the primary feature of Peshwaran. It measured 200-300 m in diameter and had brick walls standing to 17 m in height. There were at least two large towers. According to Ball there has been no significant fieldwork since the works carried out in 1936 by the DAFA survey (Delegation Archaeologique Française en Afghanistan).

name: Petaliai (islands)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 58 G2 Petaliai Inss.

name: Petit-Ménec alignments
description: An alignment of megaliths located east of Kerlescan.

name: Phaino
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 71 A4 Phaino

name: Phaistos
description: An ancient settlement on Crete where occupation began ca. 3000 B.C., Phaistos was a major center of the Minoan civilization and continued to be a Greek city of the first millennium B.C.

name: Phakoussai (islands)
description: A pair of small Aegean islands (modern Kato Koufonisi and Pano Koufonisi) located southeast of Naxos.

name: Phanagor(e)ia
description: A colony founded by Teos on the Gulf of Taman ca. 540 BC.

name: Pharmakoussai/Choirades (islands)
description: An island group, likely to be identified with the modern Nisídes Kyrádes.

name: Pheradi Maius
description: Pheradi Maius was a civitas of Africa Proconsularis. The archaeological site is today located near the village of Sidi Khelifa, Tunisia.

name: Philippaioi
description: A Hellenistic fortified site located southwest of modern Philippaioi in the municipal territory of Grevena in Greece's decentralized administration of Epirus and Western Macedonia

name: Philoteris
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 75 C2 Philoteris

name: Phintias
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 47 D4 Phintias

name: Phoenician cemetery at al-Bass
description: Cemetery at the Al-Bass site in Tyre containing burials, funerary urns, and stelae dated to the Phoenician period (8th-6th century BCE).

name: Phokoussai/Delphines (islands)
description: A group of small islands off the ancient Libyan coast, near Paraetonium and Apis. The modern Umm el-Rakham reef.

name: Phylakopi
description: A major Bronze Age settlement on Melos with evidence for continuous occupation from the third millennium BC until the twelfth century BC, Phylakopi provides important information about the Cycladic civilization.

name: Pī-Kasî
description: Site for the city of Pikasi dating back to the Old Babylonian period. A large number of cuneiform tablets were discovered there during excavations around year 2000.

name: Piana di Monte Verna
description: An ancient Oscan center delimited by a megalithic fortification wall.

name: Piazza d'Armi
description: The site of the arx of ancient Veii located on the southern spur of the volcanic plateau on which the city is located. There is archaeological evidence for an initial occupation phase of the ninth century B.C. By the seventh century B.C., the area shows evidence for adoption of rectilinear huts and assumes an orthogonal street grid.

name: Piazza d'Oro at Hadrian's Villa
description: One of the largest and most luxurious complexes of Villa Hadriani. It is situated at the northern end of the Villa, and consists of a vast building, with a vaulted, octagonal vestibule to the north, a cenatio to the south, and a central quadriporticus, down the middle of which runs a canal.

name: Piazza Pitagora
description: An in situ segment of the Roman-period paving stones of the Via Salaria was discovered in Piazza Pitagora in 2022 during utility works.

name: Pietrarossa di Trevi
description: Excavations at the archaeological site of Pietrarossa (Trebiae) revealed materials pertaining to a Roman domus in the territory of the ancient municipium. The site shows a long occupational history.

name: Pincian obelisk
description: The so-called Pincian obelisk is a monument of red granite that Hadrian brought to Rome from Egypt. It bears hieroglyphics that were likely cut at Rome and seem to indicate that the monument in some way commemorated the place where Antinous was buried. Fragments of the monument were re-erected near the Porta Maggiore in 1570. The Barberini relocated it in 1663, and it was transferred to the Vatican under Clement XIV. Pius VII transferred it to the Pincian in 1882, and it now stands in Piazza Bucarest along the Viale dell'Obelisco (Pincio).

name: Pini di Sirolo
description: "I Pini di Sirolo" is the name given to the archaeological site of a Picene necropolis of the first millennium BCE in Italy's Marche region. The necropolis included a large tumulus of the sixth century BCE that included two chariots.

name: Pinnata Castra
description: The Roman legionary fortress at modern day Inchtuthil.

name: Pir Hüseyin
description: Large mound east of Pir Hüseyin, find place for cuneiform inscription.

name: Pirabat
description: The findspot for Urartian inscribed stone blocks.

name: Piscina romana (Acqui Terme)
description: A Roman water feature at Acqui Terme dating to the Imperial period.

name: Pithekoussai (settlement)
description: In the eighth century BCE, Euboean Greeks from Eretria and Chalcis established an emporion on the island of Ischia.

name: Pitru
description: A town mentioned in Neo-Assyrian sources; it is generally identified with Aušur ar-Rafi'a (Tell Aušerīya).

name: Pityussae (islands)
description: Pityussae Inss., an ancient island group comprising the islands of Ebusus and Ophiussa.

name: Plakomenos
description: An archaeological site that yielded a rich votive deposit located south of the road leading from Nemea to Leontio, in the foothills of Mt Koukougeras on the south side of the Phliasian plain.

name: Plataea
description: Plataea is an ancient city of Boeotia, located south of Thebes. In 479 BC Plataea was the site of the Battle of Plataea, in which an allied Greek army defeated the Persians. Plataea was destroyed in the Peloponnesian War by Thebes and Sparta in 427 BC and rebuilt in 386 BC.

name: Plinthine
description: An ancient settlement at modern Kom el-Nagous in Egypt. Archaeological evidence shows habitation on the site stretches back to at least the Ramesside period. A large Ptolemaic temple seems to have been dismantled and built over during the Roman period.

name: Plotino(u)polis
description: A Roman settlement located immediately to the southeast of modern Didymoteicho, Greece, Plotinopolis was founded by the Roman emperor Trajan in honor of his wife, Plotina. Roman-era material culture has been identified at the site, beginning with chance finds in the 1960s and continuing through subsequent scientific excavation.

name: Poblado de Irulegui
description: The Iron Age settlement at Poblado de Irulegui is noted as the findspot of the so-called "Hand of Irulegi", a bronze, hand-shaped artifact dated ca. 80 to 72 BCE. This object carries an important inscription written in the Northeastern Iberian script.

name: Podere Cannicci (Civitella Paganico - GR)
description: The site of Podere Cannici has been investigated by means of both non-invasive geophysics and by excavation. Data reveal multiple phases of activity and occupation.

name: Podere Marzuolo
description: Podere Marzuolo was the site of Roman pottery production from the first century B.C. until the early fourth century A.D. The site has yielded evidence for the production of terra sigillata pottery as well as evidence of blacksmithing.

name: Podouke/Arikamedu
description: Podouke/Arikamedu is an archaeological site located in Kakkayanthope, Ariyankuppam Commune, Puducherry, India.

name: Poggio Civitate (Murlo)
description: An Etruscan elite complex of the seventh and sixth centuries B.C. located at Poggio Civitate, a hill adjacent to the medieval town of Murlo and about 25 km south of Siena.

name: Poggio Murella
description: A large, rectangular cistern (Castellum aquarum) located near Poggio Murella was built in opus reticulatum.

name: Poiessa
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 58 G2 Poiessa

name: Polonnaruwa
description: An ancient city of the Kingdom of Polonnaruwa dating ca. AD 1070. Inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1982.

name: Polyrrenia
description: Polyrrenia was an important ancient city of western Crete.

name: Pomarico Vecchio
description: A fortified center of Lucania founded in the fifth century B.C. or earlier, Pomarico Vecchio was influenced by its close contacts with the Greek cities of Magna Graecia.

name: Pompeiopolis/Sebaste
description: Pompeiopolis/Sebaste, in Paphlagonia, was founded under Pompeius in 64 B.C. in the valley of the Amnias river. The ancient site, known today as Zımbıllı Tepesi Höyüğü, is located across the river (modern Gökırmak) from modern Taşköprü in Turkey.

name: Pontia (island)
description: An island placed by Ptolemy off the coast of the Greater Syrtis, along with Maia and Misynos. The name Pontia is used in the plural by Ps. Skylax to identify all three islands as a group. These islands are identified with modern scholars as prominent parts of the modern Legarah reef (Tre Scogli) off the Libyan coast.

name: Pontiae (islands)
description: An archipelago in the Tyrrhenian Sea off the west coast of Italy.

name: Porticus Absidata
description: The Porticus Absidata was an exedra porticata that served as the northern apsidal entryway to the Forum of Nerva, also known as the Forum Transitorium.

name: Porticus Aemilia
description: A utilitarian porticus and storage building located outside the Porta Trigemina, the Porticus Aemilia was erected in 193 BCE by Marcus Aemilius Lepidus and and Lucius Aemilius Paullus.

name: Porticus Minucia
description: The Porticus Minucia was a portico that stood in the Campus Martius in Rome. It was initially built by M. Minucius Rufus, consul in 110 B.C.

name: Porticus Octaviae
description: A porticus in the area of the Circus Flaminius, the Porticus Octaviae was dedicated by Augustus sometime after 27 B.C. in honor of his sister, Octavia Minor. The porticus replaced the Porticus Metelli. It was restored twice after major urban fires in A.D. 80 and 203.

name: Porticus Philippi
description: A portico that surrounded the Temple of Hercules Musarum in the Campus Martius of ancient Rome. The construction of the portico was the result of the patronage of L. Marcius Philippus, the stepfather of Augustus.

name: Porticus Pompei
description: A porticus built in 55 B.C. in connection with the Theater of Pompey. The Curia Pompei, site of Iulius Caesar's assassination in 44 B.C., was likely located within the portico.

name: Portus
description: The great ancient maritime port of Rome, located on the right-hand bank of the Tiber River.

name: Portus Iulius
description: The so-called "Portus Iulius" is an ancient Roman port complex on the Bay of Naples. Its remains are now submerged due to the bradyseism of the Phlegraean Fields. The archaeological remains were first rediscovered in 1956.

name: Poseidonia/Paestum
description: Greeks from Sybaris originally founded Poseidonia/Paestum, a major Greco-Roman center of south Italy, in the seventh-century BCE. It was originally named Poseidonia. After the Pyrrhic war, the city became a Latin colony named Paestum ca. 273 BCE.

name: Possible heroön (Salamis)
description: A seven-room building was brought to light by underwater archaeological research in Salamis. The architectural remains may be those of a hero shrine (heroön) connected with the Greek hero Ajax.

name: Potentia
description: A city settled in an area subjugated by Rome during the fourth century B.C. The local populace rebelled against Rome after the defeat at Cannae (216 B.C.), however the defeat of the Carthaginians eventually led to Potentia's reincorporation into the Roman sphere.

name: Pottenbrunn burial ground
description: Burial site near modern Pottenbrunn, Austria, containing 43 burials dated to the Roman period.

name: Praetorian Guard Barracks at Amba Aradam Metro Station
description: Excavations for Rome's Metro C public transit line at the Amba Aradam Station revealed the remains of a second century A.D. barracks connected with the Praetorian Guard, along with a domus identified as that of a praetorian commandant. Although ruinous, the architectural complex demonstrates good preservation of some features, notably floor mosaics and wall paintings. The second century A.D. stratigraphic horizon was reached at a depth of approximately 12 meters.

name: Praetorium at Gortyna
description: The Praetorium at Gortyna dates to the first century A.D.

name: Pre-Mithraeum Building(s)
description: Fragmentary remains of three phases of pre-Roman period buildings under the foundations of the Mithraeum at Dura-Europos.

name: Priddy Circles
description: Priddy Circles describes a linear arrangement of circular earthwork enclosures near the village of Priddy, Mendip Hills, Somerset, England.

name: Priene/‘Lince’?
description: Priene/‘Lince’? was an ancient Greek city of Ionia on an escarpment of Mycale Mons.

name: Proserpina
description: A Roman-era reservoir and dam northwest of modern Mérida in Spain, which was tapped to supply Emerita Augusta (modern Mérida) via aqueduct.

name: Prummu?/Primii?
description: The one-time capital of the ancient Kerma culture in Nubia.

name: Prytanikon at Athens
description: The Prytanikon was the seat of the Prytaneis and was located on the west side of the Agora in proximity to the Old Bouleuterion, with which it formed an architectural unit. This structure was supplanted by the later Tholos and its remains are referred to as "Building F". It dates to the later period of the Peisistratids, ca. 550-525 B.C.

name: Psophis
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 58 B2 Psophis

name: Ptolemais/Barkes Limen
description: An ancient capital of Cyrenaica, Ptolemais was probably named after Ptolemy III Euergetes. The city was founded in either the seventh or sixth century BC.

name: Public administration buildings
description: The administration buildings on Pompeii's forum were built before 80 B.C. and renovated following the earthquake of A.D. 62. The central building is generally regarded as the tabularium.

name: Public building (?) area at Halos
description: According to Reinders, the slope above the lower town of Halos was probably used for public buildings.

name: Puente Tablas Iberian Oppidum
description: An Iberian settlement at the modern town of Puente Tablas (Jaén, Andalucia), with initial settlement in the late Bronze Age that continued until the 4th century BCE. The site was reoccupied during the Islamic period.

name: Puig Castellar (Biosca)
description: A hilltop fortified site southwest of modern Biosca in Catalonia, Spain. The site exhibits architecture and pottery indicating its function as a Roman military castellum during the second century BCE.

name: Pullariae (islands)
description: Pullariae Inss. (the modern the Brijuni or the Brionian Islands).

name: Punta Secca
description: An ancient settlement at Punta Secca on Sicily's southern coast. Many scholars have long identified it as a "Kaukana" mentioned by Ptolemy and Procopius, and this identification has been reflected in modern toponymy and in the scholarly literature. It has been rejected -- because there is no natural harbor at Punta Secca -- by R.J.A. Wilson in favor of an as-yet-unlocated position on the coast somewhere east of Capo Scalambri.

name: Purpurariae (islands)
description: An island group off the western coast of Morocco.

name: Puy d'Issolud
description: A Gallic site in the French commune of Vayrac, near the village of Saint-Denis-lès-Martel, often considered to be identifiable with the ancient cite of Uxellodunum, mentioned by Caesar.

name: Puzriš-Dagan
description: An ancient city of Lower Mesopotamia, Puzriš-Dagan was founded in the twenty-first century B.C. by King Shulgi of Ur.

name: Pydna
description: An ancient settlement and seaport in Macedonia, located south of modern Makrigialos. According to Apollodorus, the population was relocated to a new, inland site by Archelaos (see Pydna/Kitron), but it is clear that this original, coastal site was reoccupied.

name: Pyraloi (islands)
description: The Lamu Archipelago is located in the Indian Ocean near the northern coast of Kenya.

name: Pyramid of Djoser
description: The pyramid of the Third Dynasty pharaoh Djoser was built at Saqqara by architect Imhotep ca. 2267-2648 B.C.

name: Pyramid of Sahure
description: A pyramid complex dating to the Fifth Dynasty of Ancient Egypt and associated with the Egyptian pharaoh Sahure.

name: Pyrrichos
description: An ancient city of the Peloponnesian Mani, located near the modern village of Kavalos/Pyrrhikhos.

name: Qa`la Bost
description: Qa`la Bost is an ancient fortress within the ancient city of Bust, located on the Helmand River in southern Afghanistan and overseeing a major crossroads linking Kandahar and India to eastern Iran. Standing remains at the site predominantly date to the Islamic Middle Ages, but archaeological finds suggest an occupational history dating back to at least the first millennium BCE.

name: Qā`lah Arbīl
description: Citadel mound of modern Erbil, find place for cuneiform inscription.

name: Qal‘at ‘Awaynah
description: Stronghold with traces of Assyrian buildings where inscribed blocks have been found. Possibly ancient Alše.

name: Qal’eh-i Imam
description: Qal’eh-i Imam (“Imam Fortress”), also call Qal’eh-i Marivan (“Marivan Fortress”), is one of the most important castles and fortresses in the Kurdistan Province, Marivan District, Iran. This now-ruined military fortification played a key role during the Safavid Period (1502–1737) and was an administrative venter of Ardalanians and Babanians rulers in Kurdistan. The discovery of a stele fragment of the Assyrian king Sargon II (r. 721–705 BC) in the drystone wall enclosing one of the buildings of the village Barqaleh might attest to a settlement there in the eighth century BC.
featureTypes: fort-2, fort-group, findspot, archaeological-site, castle, settlement

name: Qalat al-Bahrain
description: An ancient settlement with evidence of occupation stretching from 2300 BC to the sixteenth century AD, Qal'at al-Bahrain was once the capital of the Dilmun civilization. In 2005, it was listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

name: Qalat-i Dinka
description: Archaeological site in the Peshdar Plain with remains dated to the Neo-Assyrian period. The mound is part of a larger frontier settlement known as the Dinka Settlement Complex.

name: Qalhat
description: An ancient city in Oman that was a key location for Indian Ocean trade networks.

name: Qaqun
description: Settlement mound from Assyrian times, with medieval ruins.

name: Qaryat ad Dawā’irah, Libya
description: A possible fortified farm site.

name: Qasr al-Mshatta
description: An Umayyad palace in Jordan, the construction of Qasr al-Mshatta is thought to have been started under Caliph Walid II around A.D. 743-44, just before the Abbassid Revolution, and it was never completed.

name: Qasr al-Sumayra
description: A late Roman fort in Egypt's Kharga Oasis.

name: Qasr el-'Al
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 71 B3 Qasr el-'Al

name: Qasr eth-Thuraiya
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 71 B3 Qasr eth-Thuraiya

name: Qasr Nessima
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 79 D3 Qasr Nessima

name: Qasr Shemamok
description: A tell located some 30 kilometers south of Erbil in Iraq's Kurdistan Region. Occupation at the site began in the second millennium BCE and continued through Parthian and Sassanian phases.

name: Qaṣr-i Abūnaṣr
description: Small mound with Achaemenian remains.

name: Qatna
description: A city in western Syria that was first settled in the Early Bronze Age III (ca. 2700-2600 BCE). In the Middle Bronze Age (2000-1600 BCE), it became a major trading center and the capital of a regional kingdom. In the Late Bronze Age, the city of Qatna became a vassal of first the Mitanni Kingdom, then the Neo-Hittite Empire, and Egypt. The city's acropolis was abandoned in the Late Bronze Age I, though the surrounding area remained inhabited. It was resettled in the 10th century BCE as part of the Aramaean kingdom of Hamath, but was sacked by the army of Sargon II in 720 and ultimately abandoned in the 6th century BCE.

name: Qattara?/Karana?
description: Tell al-Rimah, is the site of a settlement inhabited from the third to first millennium BCE that has been identified as the site of either Qattara or Karana.

name: Qermez Dere
description: A tell located in Tal Afar in Nineveh Governorate, Iraq, that was the site of a Neolithic settlement established ca. 8500-7900 BCE. Neolithic roundhouses were documented at the site.

name: Quadriporticus of the theatre
description: A four-sided portico standing behind the large theater of Pompeii (VIII, 7, 16-17). The complex was constructed ca. 80 BC.

name: Quartier du Stade at Delos
description: Neighborhood surrounding the stadium in the settlement of Delos, constructed during the Hellenistic period.

name: Qurghan tepe
description: Identified by W. Ball as "Qurghan tepe, Imām Ṣāhib" and distinct from other similar toponyms.

name: Qutb Minar
description: The second tallest minar (73 m) in India after Minar-E-Fateh was built by Qutb-ud-din Aibak beginning in AD 1192. The monument is listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

name: Rabat Tapah
description: FInd place for cuneiform inscription.

name: Rās al-Maṭbakh
description: Find place for cuneiform inscription.

name: Ras Ibn Hani
description: Archaeological evidence on this cape on the northwest coast of the modern country of Syria demonstrates continuous human occupation from the Bronze Age through Byzantine times, down to the modern day.

name: Razliq
description: Razliq is a village situated ca. 13 km north of the city Sarāb in Eastern Azerbaijan (Iran). Here a rock inscription of the Urartian king Argišti II, son of Rusa (end of 8th / first half of 7th century BCE), has been found which reports military campaigns of Argišti II to this region. As well as the rock inscription of Nashteban (CTU A 11-5) it is situated on the southern slope of the Sabalān Mountains at an altitude of 4810 m. Although the inscription reports the building of a fortress named ‘stronghold of Argišti’ no such archaeological remains have been discovered so far. Together with the rock inscriptions found in Nashteban (CTU A 11-5) and Shisheh (CTU A 11-6) the rock inscription found near Razliq is one of the Urartian inscriptions found furthest to the east.

name: Regia
description: The Regia is an ancient complex along the Sacra Via in Rome at the margin of the Forum Romanum, adjacent to the Temple of Vesta. It is understood as a sort of ritual headquarters of Rome's early kings and, later, as a structure connected with the office of the pontifex maximus.

name: Regio V, Pompeii
description: The so-called Regio V, a largely unexcavated archaeological subdivision located in the northern section of Pompeii, contains a mixture of commercial and domestic structures, including a gladiators' barracks.

name: Residential Palace ("P")
description: Residential Palace ("P") is often regarded as the residence of Cyrus the Great at Pasargadae.

name: Review Palace
description: An Assyrian armory was built to the south of the citadel at Nineveh. The ruins of the 7th-century B.C. architectural complex are directly below the modern settlement built on top of Nebi Yunus, the site's smaller mound.

name: Rhaithou
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 76 D5 Rhaithou

name: Rhamnous
description: Rhamnous was an ancient settlement of Attica adjacent to the Euboean Strait and a coastal deme of the tribe Aiantis.

name: Rhapta
description: A city and trading port on the east coast of Africa, mentioned in the Periplus of the Erythraean Sea and other sources.
